query: effectiveness of selumetinib in treating low-grade glioma

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Targeted therapies such as BRAF/MEK inhibitors may be appropriate in certain gliomas, such as pilocytic astrocytomas, pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA), and ganglioneuromas. Patients that are incompletely resected or have progression with a BRAF V600E activating mutations may be eligible for Dabrafenib/trametinib or vemurafenib/cobimetinib. These drugs arrest cell growth by inhibiting B-Raf (dabrafenib and vemurafenib) and MEK (trametinib and cobimetinib), which are part of the Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK pathway that stimulates cell proliferation. For subependymal giant cell astrocytomas, an mTOR inhibitor such as everolimus may be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Nerve Glioma -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Targeted therapies focusing on the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway have become more prevalent, emphasizing the importance of tumor biopsies. The most common genetic aberration is a BRAF-KIAA1549 proto-oncogene fusion, which activates the MEK/MAPK/ERK and mTOR pathways and causes cell proliferation, survival, and tumorigenesis. MEK inhibitors such as selumetinib, trametinib, and refametinib target this pathway and report a 69% 2-year progression-free survival for refractory optic pathway gliomas. [43] Trametinib is currently the most widely used MEK inhibitor, although more than a dozen alternatives are undergoing clinical trials. [44] These agents represent a viable salvage alternative for optic pathway gliomas that have progressed through other systemic agents. The most common adverse effects of MEK inhibitors involve dermatologic conditions; however, vision-threatening ocular adverse events, including optic neuropathy, retinal vein occlusion, uveitis, neurosensory retinal detachment, and MEK inhibitor-associated retinopathy, have been reported. Fortunately, these events are rare and often reversible, particularly in pediatric patients. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The number of systemic agents available for use in low-grade gliomas is quite limited. This is at least partly due to the blood-brain barrier, which prevents higher concentrations of these agents in the CNS. Targeted therapies also have a role in treating some of these malignancies.

retrieved_docs.content: On further follow-up, the 5 and 10-year overall survival was 60.9% and 34.6%. [48] The drug is delivered at 75mg/m given with radiotherapy and 150mg/m post-radiation. The most common toxicity is myelosuppression, as well as nausea and vomiting.

retrieved_docs.title: Leptomeningeal Carcinomatosis -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Bevacizumab (VGEF inhibitor) and dabrafenib (BRAF inhibitor) have been reported to demonstrate a response in LC from melanoma. IT trastuzumab in LC from HER-2 positive breast cancer also has shown some promise and a favorable adverse effect profile. There are several ongoing phase II trials on the subject. EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) has shown a response to erlotinib and gefitinib, but at higher doses, as they do not cross the BBB easily. There are ongoing trials of other tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) in LC from EGFR-mutant NSCLC that have shown promising results in terms of extended survival. Anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) inhibitors are another class of drugs shown to be effective in LC from NSCLC with ALK rearrangements in ongoing trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- Neurological
retrieved_docs.content: Cognitive impairment Peripheral neuropathy Headaches Dizziness

retrieved_docs.title: Gliomas -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: As many pediatric low-grade gliomas have genomic alterations in the mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway, studies have focused on targeted therapies involving this pathway. The phase 2 FIREFLY-1 trial assessed the efficacy of the RAF inhibitor tovorafenib in patients who suffered from relapse or refractory low-grade gliomas with BRAF alterations. [22] This study demonstrated an overall response rate of 67% and 51% when assessed using the Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology (RANO) for high-grade gliomas (HGG) and Response Assessment in Pediatric Neuro-Oncology (RAPNO) criteria, respectively. Furthermore, the median time to treatment response was 3.0 months and 5.3 months per RANO-HGG and RAPNO criteria, respectively. These findings provide encouraging evidence for another treatment option for patients with relapsed or refractory low-grade gliomas. Several other studies have shown promising results with chemotherapy-directed treatment for diffuse midline gliomas, H3K27M altered. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Astrocytoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Recommendations for recurrent tumors include reoperation, carmustine wafers, alternate chemotherapeutic regimens, and investigational IDH -targeted approaches. Re-radiation is rarely helpful; an additional 36 Gy in 18 fractions may be given. Bevacizumab, a humanized VEGF monoclonal antibody, has activity in recurrent glioblastoma, potentially increasing progression-free survival (PFS) and reducing peritumoral edema and glucocorticoid use. [12] IDH -targeted approaches, including vorasidenib and other small-molecule inhibitors of mutant IDH1 and 2 (ivosidenib, olutasidenib, safusedinib), are in trials. Vaccine strategies are also being explored. Recurrent glioblastoma treatment decisions must take into consideration factors such as previous therapy, time to relapse, performance status, and quality of life. Whenever possible, patients with recurrent disease should be enrolled in clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.content: Consider enrolling eligible patients in clinical trials to access cutting-edge therapies and contribute to advancing treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinomatous Meningitis -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) tyrosine kinase inhibitors (EGFR-TKI) are used in lung cancers with EGFR mutations. [44] Erlotinib, gefitinib, and osimertinib are EGFR inhibitors. HER2 TKI is used in breast cancers. Lapatinib and tucatinib are examples. [45] BRAF inhibitors like vemurafenib have shown good clinical response and improved survival with leptomeningeal metastasis in patients with melanoma. [46] Anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) inhibitors are used in lung cancers with ALK rearrangements. Drugs include crizotinib, ceritinib, alectinib, brigatinib, and lorlatinib. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Gliomas -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment of Gliomas
retrieved_docs.content: Surgery: Surgical resection is the mainstay of treatment with a goal of maximal safe resection, depending on tumor grading and location. Grade I: Most of these gliomas are surgically curable; however, in some cases, such as optic-pathway gliomas, surgical resection may be limited. Grade II: Current acceptable practices include a maximal safe resection and the possibility of gross total resection, with radiographic follow-up. Grade III: Maximal safe resection, concomitant chemoradiation, and radiographic follow-up for recurrence is an acceptable treatment. Grade IV: Maximal safe resection, concomitant chemoradiation, and radiographic follow-up for recurrence is an acceptable treatment. In select cases, as in diffuse midline gliomas, surgery is often limited to a stereotactic biopsy for molecular characterization.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuronal Brain Tumors -- Treatment / Management -- Chemo- and Immunotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Chemo- and immuno-therapies are typically reserved for grade 2 and 3 tumors in a fashion similar to radiation therapy. Immunotherapies targeting BRAF are emerging as adjuvant therapy for use in anaplastic ganglioglioma. [44] [59] [60] Examples of BRAF inhibitors include vemurafenib, dabrafenib, and encorafenib. The use of chemotherapy in central neurocytoma is variable and typically reserved for patients with recurrent tumors. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” comprises the kinase‑targeted agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin. Both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated as potential therapies for acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). As members of the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, these drugs share a common mechanism of action that involves inhibition of specific signaling pathways driving malignant cell proliferation, and their use in AML highlights the therapeutic relevance of kinase inhibition in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1290
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and gilteritinib are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group designated “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” indicating that each drug belongs to this specific category of protein kinase–targeting agents. This classification reflects their shared mechanism of action as protein kinase inhibitors within the broader ATC system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: lymphoma cells; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor, enhances antitumor immune responses; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, can augment host immunity. Collectively, these agents—ranging from traditional chemotherapeutics and central‑nervous‑system depressants to modern immunotherapies—illustrate the comprehensive pharmacologic approach to Hodgkin disease, emphasizing individualized regimen selection to optimize patient outcomes and underscoring the importance of systematic drug classification for clinical research and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1289
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat سرطـان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia), offering a pharmacologic option for managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 425
retrieved_docs.content: the experimental compound PD‑134308 functions simultaneously as a hormonal antagonist and an antitumor agent directed against the target isa, exemplifying dual pharmacologic activity. This integrated framework delineates classifications, qualifying relationships, therapeutic scopes and specific contraindications of key antineoplastic and inhibitory drugs, supporting informed oncologic patient care.
